question: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is:

options.A: enveloped DNA virus without reverse transcriptase
options.B: non-enveloped RNA virus with reverse transcriptase
options.C: enveloped DNA virus with reverse transcriptase
options.D: enveloped RNA virus with reverse transcriptase

explanation:
HIV is a retrovirus of the family Retroviridae whose genome consists of two identical single-stranded, positive-sense RNA molecules packaged inside a capsid, which in turn is surrounded by a lipid envelope studded with gp120/gp41 glycoproteins. The virion carries its own reverse transcriptase (an RNA-dependent DNA polymerase) that transcribes viral RNA into proviral DNA in the host cell.

Option A (enveloped DNA virus without reverse transcriptase): HIV has no DNA genome and does carry reverse transcriptase.

Option B (non-enveloped RNA virus with reverse transcriptase): HIV is enveloped; the envelope is acquired by budding through the host plasma membrane.

Option C (enveloped DNA virus with reverse transcriptase): HIV is not a DNA virus.

question: Highly infective viral or bacterial pathogens are called:
explanation:
Virulence is the quantitative measure of a pathogen's capacity to cause disease, encompassing both infectivity (ability to establish infection) and severity of the disease produced; highly infective pathogens are therefore, by definition, virulent.

Option A (opportunistic): Opportunistic pathogens cause disease only when host defences are compromised, so they are the opposite of highly infective.

Option B (symbiotic): Symbiotic organisms live in mutually beneficial or at least non-harmful association with the host and are not classified as pathogens at all.

Option D (parasitic): Parasitic merely describes a nutritional relationship in which one organism benefits at the host's expense and says nothing about infectivity.

question: Pinocytosis is:

options.B: cell uptake of droplets of extracellular fluid
options.C: cell excretion of droplets of extracellular fluid

explanation:
Pinocytosis ("cell drinking") is a form of endocytosis in which the plasma membrane invaginates to enclose small droplets of extracellular fluid and their dissolved solutes, forming pinocytotic vesicles that are internalised non-selectively.

Option A (cell uptake of solid particles): describes phagocytosis, the uptake of large solid particles such as bacteria by cells like macrophages and neutrophils.

Option C (cell excretion of droplets of extracellular...): describe exocytosis (secretion of fluid) and the egestion of undigested residues, respectively; both are outward-directed processes and not pinocytosis, which is strictly inward transport.

Option D (cell excretion of solid particles): describe exocytosis (secretion of fluid) and the egestion of undigested residues, respectively; both are outward-directed processes and not pinocytosis, which is strictly inward transport.

question: The structure of eukaryotic flagellum contains:
explanation:
The eukaryotic flagellum is built around an axoneme with the classical 9+2 arrangement of microtubule doublets, and microtubules are polymers of α- and β-tubulin heterodimers.

Option A (proteins called flagellins): refers to flagellins, which are the protein subunits of the entirely different bacterial flagellum (a rotating helical filament, not a microtubule structure).

Option B (proteins called lamelins): is a distractor: "lamelins" is not a real protein family (the similar-sounding lamins form the nuclear lamina, not flagella).

Option C (proteins called kinesins): kinesins, are microtubule-associated motor proteins that walk along microtubules but are not structural components of the flagellum; the flagellar motor protein is dynein, which drives bending of the axoneme.

question: Function of rough endoplasmatic reticulum is:

options.B: synthesis and secretion of proteins and glycoproteins
options.C: sorting of all proteins in cell

explanation:
The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) is studded with membrane-bound ribosomes that co-translationally translocate nascent polypeptides bearing signal sequences into the ER lumen, where they undergo folding, disulphide-bond formation and N-linked glycosylation before being packaged into COPII vesicles for delivery to the Golgi and ultimate secretion.

Option A (synthesis of lipids): describes the smooth ER, which lacks ribosomes and specialises in lipid/steroid synthesis and detoxification.

Option C (sorting of all proteins in cell): is the job of the Golgi apparatus, which sorts proteins to lysosomes, secretory vesicles or the plasma membrane.

Option D (sorting and secretion of polysaccharides): wrong on two counts: polysaccharides are not the major cargo of the ER, and sorting is a Golgi function.

question: G0 phase of cell life cycle corresponds to:
explanation:
G0 is a quiescent, non-dividing state in which cells exit the active cell cycle from G1; most terminally differentiated cells (neurons, cardiomyocytes) and many resting lymphocytes reside permanently or reversibly in G0.

Option A (high proliferation phase): describes actively cycling cells, which are in G1/S/G2/M rather than G0.

Option B (preparation phase for mitosis): is G2, when the cell has completed DNA replication and prepares proteins and organelles required for mitosis.

Option C (synthesis phase, replication of DNA): is the S phase, defined by DNA replication.

question: Enzyme Primase is:
explanation:
Primase synthesises short RNA primers (~10 nucleotides) on the single-stranded DNA template so that DNA polymerases, which cannot initiate synthesis de novo, have a 3'-OH from which to extend; it therefore reads DNA and produces RNA, i.e., it is a DNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

Option A (DNA-dependent DNA polymerase): describes conventional replicative DNA polymerases that copy DNA into DNA.

Option C (RNA-dependent RNA polymerase): describes viral RNA replicases such as those of RNA viruses.

Option D (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase): describes reverse transcriptase, which copies RNA into DNA.

question: Introns are:
explanation:
Introns are the intervening, non-coding stretches of a eukaryotic gene that are transcribed into the primary transcript (pre-mRNA) and then removed by the spliceosome; the remaining exons are ligated to produce the mature mRNA.

Option B (coding regions of primary transcripts): the coding stretches of the primary transcript are the exons, not the introns.

Option C (non-coding regions of mRNAs): by the time an mRNA is mature, the introns have already been excised, so introns are not part of the mRNA at all.

Option D (coding regions of mRNAs): by the time an mRNA is mature, the introns have already been excised, so introns are not part of the mRNA at all.

question: In Mendel's experiments the number of gamete types in trihybrid cross:
explanation:
By the law of independent assortment, a heterozygote at n unlinked loci produces 2n gamete types; for a trihybrid (AaBbCc), this gives 23 = 8 combinations (ABC, ABc, AbC, Abc, aBC, aBc, abC, abc).

Option A (2): (2) is the number of gamete types in a monohybrid (21).

Option B (4): (4) is the answer for a dihybrid (22).

Option C (6): (6) does not correspond to any 2n value and reflects the common error of multiplying 3×2 instead of raising 2 to the third power.

question: Thomas Hunt Morgan described:
explanation:
Working with Drosophila melanogaster, Morgan established the chromosome theory of inheritance and, most famously, demonstrated gene linkage: genes located on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together, and their recombination frequency reflects their physical distance, a principle later used by Sturtevant to build the first genetic maps.

Option A (independent assortment of chromosomes): is Mendel's second law, formulated well before Morgan and describing unlinked loci.

Option C (gene regulation in Drosophila melanogaster): alludes to work on lac operon-style gene regulation done decades later by Jacob and Monod (in bacteria, not flies).

Option D (structure of sex chromosomes): was worked out through cytogenetic studies that Morgan contributed to but is not what he is chiefly remembered for; his 1933 Nobel Prize was awarded specifically for linkage and the role of chromosomes in heredity.

question: The phenotype ratio of F<sub>2</sub> generation in Mendelian monohybrid cross for the incomplete dominance inheritance is defined as:
explanation:
Under incomplete dominance the heterozygote has an intermediate phenotype (classically pink flowers from red × white parents), so genotype and phenotype ratios coincide: F2 segregates as 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa, giving the phenotype ratio 1 : 2 : 1.

Option A (1 : 3): are variants of the classical Mendelian dominance ratio, which arises only when Aa is phenotypically identical to AA.

Option B (3 : 1): are variants of the classical Mendelian dominance ratio, which arises only when Aa is phenotypically identical to AA.

Option C (1 : 1): (1:1) is the ratio expected from a test cross (Aa × aa), not an F2 from monohybrid selfing.

question: The human secondary oocyte, the result of meiosis I, contains:
explanation:
Meiosis I is a reductional division: it separates homologous chromosomes but each chromosome still consists of the two sister chromatids generated in the preceding S phase. The secondary oocyte is therefore haploid in chromosome number (n = 23) but its DNA content is still 2c, i.e., 46 chromatids.

Option A (23 chromosomes and 23 chromatids): confuses meiosis I with meiosis II (which separates sister chromatids to give 23 chromosomes/23 chromatids).

Option C (46 chromosomes and 23 chromatids): wrongly state 46 chromosomes, which is the diploid count of somatic cells or of the primary oocyte before meiosis I.

Option D (46 chromosomes and 46 chromatids): wrongly state 46 chromosomes, which is the diploid count of somatic cells or of the primary oocyte before meiosis I.

question: The gene mutation caused by loss of two nucleotides leads to:

options.A: loss of normal amino acid alignment behind the mutation
options.B: loss of one amino acid at the protein level
options.C: loss of two amino acids at the protein level
options.D: loss of three amino acids at the protein level

explanation:
Because the genetic code is read in non-overlapping triplets, deleting a number of nucleotides that is not a multiple of three shifts the reading frame; losing two nucleotides produces a classical frameshift mutation in which every codon downstream of the deletion is misread, so all subsequent amino acids are wrong and a premature stop codon is usually generated.

Option B (loss of one amino acid at...): describe simple in-frame losses of one, two or three residues, which would require the deletion of 3, 6 or 9 nucleotides respectively (multiples of three preserve the reading frame).

Option C (loss of two amino acids at...): describe simple in-frame losses of one, two or three residues, which would require the deletion of 3, 6 or 9 nucleotides respectively (multiples of three preserve the reading frame).

Option D (loss of three amino acids at...): describe simple in-frame losses of one, two or three residues, which would require the deletion of 3, 6 or 9 nucleotides respectively (multiples of three preserve the reading frame). Since only 2 nucleotides are lost here, the amino acid sequence downstream is scrambled rather than shortened by a defined number of residues.

question: Unbalanced rearrangements of chromosomes DO NOT include:
explanation:
A reciprocal translocation is the mutual exchange of segments between two non-homologous chromosomes; no genetic material is gained or lost, so it is a balanced rearrangement (carriers are usually phenotypically normal but at risk of unbalanced gametes).

Option B (isochromosomes): an isochromosome, arises when a chromosome divides transversely through its centromere, giving one arm duplicated and the other lost, which is intrinsically unbalanced.

Option C (deletions): deletions and duplications, by definition remove or add genetic material and are therefore always unbalanced.

Option D (duplications): deletions and duplications, by definition remove or add genetic material and are therefore always unbalanced.

question: When there are two alleles on a gene (dominant A and recessive a), then the probability (p) of dominant phenotype is:
explanation:
A dominant phenotype is expressed whenever at least one dominant allele is present, so it arises from both the homozygous dominant (AA) and the heterozygous (Aa) genotypes; its probability is therefore the sum of these two genotype frequencies, pAA + pAa.

Option A (pA): (pA) is only the allele frequency, not the phenotype frequency.

Option B (pAA): (pAA) omits the heterozygotes, who also show the dominant phenotype.

Option D (pAa): (pAa) counts only the heterozygotes and ignores AA homozygotes.

question: In the population at equilibrium, on a gene with 2 alleles; the frequency of dominant allele is 60%, the frequency of recessive allele is 40%. What is the frequency of heterozygotes?
explanation:
Under Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, genotype frequencies are p2 + 2pq + q2 = 1, so heterozygote frequency = 2pq = 2 × 0.6 × 0.4 = 0.48.

Option A (0.50): (0.50) is a rounded but incorrect value.

Option C (0.25): (0.25) confuses the calculation with the F2 heterozygote fraction of a Mendelian monohybrid cross (which assumes p = q = 0.5, not 0.6/0.4).

Option D (0.24): (0.24) is the product pq without the factor of 2 that accounts for the two possible parental origins of the two alleles.

question: Which sequence of biological formations is from the most complex to the simplest ones?
explanation:
The hierarchical organisation of a multicellular body descends: organ system → organ → tissue → cell.

Option B (cells – tissues – organs –...): is the correct hierarchy but in the opposite direction (simple to complex).

Option C (organ systems – organisms – tissues...): wrongly interposes "organism" (a higher level than organ system in one direction, or a whole above it) between organ systems and tissues, breaking the ordering.

Option D (organs – organ systems – organisms...): scrambles the levels entirely (organs above organ systems, then organisms, then tissues).

question: Animal tissues fall into:
explanation:
Classical histology recognises four fundamental animal tissue types: epithelial (covering and secretion), connective (support, including blood, bone, cartilage, adipose), muscle (contractile: skeletal, cardiac, smooth) and nervous (excitable).

Option A (three main categories): undercounts by omitting one of these four categories.

Option C (five main categories): over-count and would require splitting one of the four (for example, treating blood or adipose as separate primary categories, which is not standard).

Option D (six main categories): over-count and would require splitting one of the four (for example, treating blood or adipose as separate primary categories, which is not standard).

question: Nerve system originates from:
explanation:
During neurulation the dorsal ectoderm thickens into the neural plate, folds up to form the neural tube (central nervous system) and gives rise to the neural crest, which generates most of the peripheral nervous system, so the entire nervous system is of ectodermal origin.

Option A (mesoderm): mesoderm, forms muscle, bone, connective tissue, kidneys, gonads and the circulatory system.

Option B (endoderm): endoderm, forms the epithelial linings of the gut, lungs and associated glands (liver, pancreas).

Option D (combined germ layers): is incorrect because the nervous system arises from a single germ layer, not a combination.

question: The human heart is composed for:

options.B: double circulation with mixed oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
options.C: double circulation with separated oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
options.D: quartet circulation with separated oxygenated and deoxygenated blood

explanation:
Mammals, including humans, have a four-chambered heart that drives a double circulation: the right side pumps deoxygenated blood through the pulmonary circuit while the left side pumps oxygenated blood through the systemic circuit, and the two streams are kept completely separated by the interatrial and interventricular septa.

Option A (single circulation): describes fish, which have a two-chambered heart and a single circuit.

Option B (double circulation with mixed oxygenated and...): describes amphibians and most reptiles, whose three-chambered hearts allow partial mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.

Option D (quartet circulation with separated oxygenated and...): ("quartet circulation") is not an anatomical category.

question: Muscles contraction:

options.A: requires energy in the form of ATP
options.B: requires energy in the form of GTP
options.D: requires energy in the form of oxygen

explanation:
The cross-bridge cycle requires ATP at two steps: ATP binding to myosin detaches the head from actin, and ATP hydrolysis re-cocks the myosin head for the next power stroke; ATP is also needed by the sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase to relax the muscle.

Option B (requires energy in the form of...): GTP, is used by G proteins and in protein synthesis (translocation on the ribosome), not by the myosin motor.

Option C (does not require energy): is refuted by rigor mortis, in which depletion of ATP leaves myosin bound to actin and the muscle rigid.

Option D (requires energy in the form of...): confuses fuel with energy currency: oxygen is required for aerobic ATP regeneration but is not itself the energy source used by the contractile machinery.

question: In the human kidney, the osmotic gradient, that concentrates the urine, is present in:

options.A: the proximal tubule and the loop of Henle
options.B: the distal tubule and the collecting duct
options.D: the loop of Henle and the collecting duct

explanation:
The countercurrent multiplier in the loop of Henle establishes a corticomedullary osmotic gradient by pumping NaCl out of the thick ascending limb while water leaves the descending limb, driving medullary osmolality up to ~1200 mOsm/kg; the collecting duct then traverses this gradient and, under the influence of ADH (which inserts aquaporin-2 channels), reabsorbs water to concentrate the urine.

Option A (the proximal tubule and the loop...): the proximal tubule reabsorbs iso-osmotically and does not build a gradient.

Option B (the distal tubule and the collecting...): is incomplete: the distal tubule does fine-tune reabsorption but the gradient itself is not built there.

Option C (the proximal and distal tubules): omits both structures that actually generate and exploit the gradient.

question: In the testes, nourishment of developing sperm is mediated through:
explanation:
Sertoli cells line the seminiferous tubules, form the blood–testis barrier via tight junctions, and directly nurse the developing germ cells by providing nutrients, growth factors, androgen-binding protein and inhibin, while phagocytosing residual bodies.

Option B (Cowper's cells): Cowper's (bulbourethral) glands, secrete pre-ejaculate mucus into the urethra and are not intratubular cells.

Option C (follicular cells): follicular cells, belong to the ovary (surrounding the oocyte) or the thyroid, not the testis.

Option D (Leydig cells): Leydig cells, sit in the interstitium and synthesise testosterone; they support spermatogenesis hormonally but do not directly nourish the germ cells.

question: The hormone glucagon is produce by:
explanation:
Glucagon is a 29-amino-acid peptide hormone secreted by the α (A) cells of the pancreatic islets of Langerhans; it opposes insulin and raises blood glucose by stimulating hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis.

Option A (anterior pituitary): the anterior pituitary, produces tropic hormones (ACTH, TSH, GH, LH, FSH, prolactin), not glucagon.

Option B (thyroid gland): the thyroid, secretes T3/T4 and calcitonin.

Option C (adrenal glands): the adrenal glands, produce catecholamines (medulla) and steroid hormones (cortex); although adrenaline also raises blood glucose, it is not glucagon.

question: Which event DOES NOT correlate with sympathetic nervous activity?
explanation:
The sympathetic system mediates the "fight-or-flight" response and inhibits gastrointestinal motility and secretion; peristalsis is stimulated by the parasympathetic ("rest-and-digest") system via the vagus nerve.

Option A (heartbeat acceleration): is a classic sympathetic effect: β1-adrenergic stimulation of the SA node increases heart rate and contractility.

Option C (pupil dilatation): mydriasis, is produced by sympathetic activation of the radial pupillary muscle via α1 receptors.

Option D (secretion of adrenalin and noradrenalin): describes the adrenal medulla, which is essentially a modified sympathetic ganglion and releases adrenaline and noradrenaline on sympathetic stimulation.

question: Osmotic pressure:

options.A: a measure of the tendency of a solution to take up glucose when separated from pure water by a selectively permeable membrane
options.B: a measure of the tendency of a solution to take up sodium when separated from pure water by a selectively permeable membrane
options.C: a measure of the tendency of a solution to take up kalium when separated from pure water by a selectively permeable membrane
options.D: a measure of the tendency of a solution to take up water when separated from pure water by a selectively permeable membrane

explanation:
Osmotic pressure is the hydrostatic pressure that must be applied to a solution to prevent the net influx of water across a semipermeable membrane from a compartment of pure solvent; quantitatively it is given by van't Hoff's equation Π = iMRT. A semipermeable membrane by definition allows solvent (water) but not solute to cross, so

Option A (a measure of the tendency of...): those solutes cannot pass through the membrane, and osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water.

Option B (a measure of the tendency of...): those solutes cannot pass through the membrane, and osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water.

Option C (a measure of the tendency of...): those solutes cannot pass through the membrane, and osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water. The solution takes up water, not solutes.

question: The cochlea, the organ of hearing, has:

options.A: the large vestibular and tympanic canals containing endolymph, and the small cochlear duct containing perilymph and the Organ of Corti
options.B: the large vestibular and tympanic canals containing endolymph and the Organ of Corti, and the small cochlear duct containing perilymph
options.C: the large vestibular and tympanic canals containing perilymph, and the small cochlear duct containing endolymph and the Organ of Corti
options.D: the large vestibular and tympanic canals containing perilymph and the Organ of Corti, and the small cochlear duct containing endolymph

explanation:
The cochlea has three fluid-filled compartments: the scala vestibuli and scala tympani both contain perilymph (Na+-rich, similar to extracellular fluid), while the intervening scala media (cochlear duct) contains endolymph (K+-rich, secreted by the stria vascularis) and houses the Organ of Corti on the basilar membrane.

Option A (the large vestibular and tympanic canals...): misassign endolymph to the vestibular/tympanic scalae.

Option B (the large vestibular and tympanic canals...): misassign endolymph to the vestibular/tympanic scalae.

Option D (the large vestibular and tympanic canals...): correctly identifies perilymph in the large canals but wrongly places the Organ of Corti there rather than in the cochlear duct.

question: In the depolarization phase of the nerve action potential:
explanation:
Once threshold is reached, voltage-gated Na+ channels open, allowing a rapid inward Na+ current that drives the membrane potential from about −70 mV toward +30 mV; this is the depolarisation phase of the axonal action potential.

Option B (Li gates are open): neurons possess no lithium-selective channels; Li+ is used therapeutically but has no physiological ion channel of its own.

Option C (Ca gates are open): a2+, opens later at presynaptic terminals to trigger neurotransmitter release and drives cardiac plateau potentials, but not the depolarisation of the axonal action potential.

Option D (Mn gates are open): is a distractor: there are no manganese-gated ion channels in excitable membranes.

question: Blood plasma contains:
explanation:
Human plasma is approximately 90–92% water and 8–10% dissolved solids: mainly plasma proteins (~7 g/dL: albumin, globulins, fibrinogen), electrolytes, nutrients, hormones and metabolic waste.

Option B (80% water and 20% dry matter): (80/20) roughly matches whole blood, in which the cellular fraction lowers water content, but the question asks about plasma alone.

Option C (70% water and 30% dry matter): drastically underestimate the water fraction and would be inconsistent with plasma being a fluid capable of carrying dissolved solutes.

Option D (60% water and 40% dry matter): drastically underestimate the water fraction and would be inconsistent with plasma being a fluid capable of carrying dissolved solutes.

question: Water-soluble vitamin:
explanation:
Folic acid (vitamin B9) is a B-complex vitamin and, like all B vitamins and vitamin C, is water-soluble; its polar pteridine, p-aminobenzoate and glutamate moieties confer high aqueous solubility, and excess is excreted in urine.

Option A (A): are the classic fat-soluble vitamins A, E and K (together with vitamin D, forming the mnemonic ADEK); they are absorbed with dietary lipids and stored in liver and adipose tissue.

Option B (E): are the classic fat-soluble vitamins A, E and K (together with vitamin D, forming the mnemonic ADEK); they are absorbed with dietary lipids and stored in liver and adipose tissue.

Option D (K): are the classic fat-soluble vitamins A, E and K (together with vitamin D, forming the mnemonic ADEK); they are absorbed with dietary lipids and stored in liver and adipose tissue.

question: Oxidation is an important reaction type in the human metabolism. Which of the following chemical conversions is an example of oxidation?
explanation:
Conversion of a secondary alcohol (CH3-CH(OH)-CH2-COOH, β-hydroxybutyrate) to a ketone (CH3-CO-CH2-COOH, acetoacetate) removes two hydrogen atoms (dehydrogenation) and formally raises the oxidation state of the carbinol carbon from −1 to +2, which is by definition an oxidation.

Option A (CH3-CHO → CH3-CH2-OH): is a reduction (acetaldehyde → ethanol, adds 2 H).

Option C (CH3-COOH → CH3-COO− + H+): is a simple acid dissociation (loss of H+, no change in oxidation state).

Option D (CH3-(CH2)7-CH=CH-(CH2)7-COOH → CH3-(CH2)16-COOH): is hydrogenation of a C=C double bond (oleic → stearic acid), which is another reduction.

question: Molecules that are poorly soluble in water require carrier proteins for their transport in blood. Choose an example of such a molecule:
explanation:
Stearic acid (CH3-(CH2)16-COOH) is a saturated C18 fatty acid whose long hydrophobic hydrocarbon tail vastly outweighs the polar carboxylate head, giving it negligible water solubility; in plasma it circulates non-covalently bound to albumin.

Option A (lactic acid): lactic acid, is a small α-hydroxy acid, fully water-soluble and freely dissolved in plasma.

Option B (glucose): glucose, has five hydroxyl groups and is one of the most water-soluble metabolites; it needs GLUT transporters to cross membranes but not carriers in plasma.

Option C (acetone): acetone, is a small polar ketone miscible with water and transported dissolved.

question: In cells, carbohydrates are first "activated" before they enter metabolic conversions. The "activated" forms of carbohydrates are often esters of phosphoric acid. Select the structure describing a phosphorylated carbohydrate (drawing the structure in full can help you):
explanation:
Structure A is glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P): a three-carbon aldose (CHO–CHOH–CH2OH) whose C3 hydroxyl is esterified with phosphoric acid, making it a genuine phosphorylated carbohydrate and a key glycolytic intermediate.

Option B (HO-CH2-CH(OH)-CO-O-PO-(OH)2): has a keto group next to the phosphate, but the phosphate is bonded through a C=O carbon (a mixed anhydride, not a carbohydrate ester).

Option C ((HO)2-PO-O-PO-(OH)2): (HO)2PO–O–PO(OH)2, is pyrophosphate, an inorganic species with no carbohydrate moiety at all.

Option D (HO-CH2-CH(OH)-CH2-O-PO-(OH)2): lacks a carbonyl (no CHO or C=O), so it is a phosphorylated polyol (glycerol-1-phosphate), not a carbohydrate.

question: Common unsaturated fatty acids occurring in the human body have an even number of carbons and one or more cis double bond(s). Which of the following molecules is an example of fatty acid that can exist in the cis configuration?
explanation:
cis/trans isomerism requires a C=C double bond with two different substituents on each sp2 carbon. The suffix "-en-" in octadecenoic acid denotes exactly one such double bond in an 18-carbon chain; the physiologically relevant isomer is cis-9-octadecenoic acid, i.e., oleic acid.

Option A (butanoic acid): (butanoic, C4 saturated),.

Option B (butanedioic acid): (butanedioic acid, i.e., succinic acid, saturated dicarboxylic) and.

Option D (hexadecanoic acid): (hexadecanoic acid, i.e., palmitic acid, C16 saturated) contain no C=C double bond, so cis/trans isomerism is impossible.

question: The chemical conversion of ethanol to ethanal can be described as
explanation:
Ethanol (CH3-CH2-OH) becomes ethanal (CH3-CHO) by the removal of two hydrogen atoms (one from the α-carbon, one from the hydroxyl), a dehydrogenation that is biochemically the classic NAD+-dependent oxidation catalysed by alcohol dehydrogenase.

Option A (addition of water): (addition of water) would give a hydrate/geminal diol, not an aldehyde.

Option B (elimination of water): (elimination of water) would give ethene, not ethanal.

Option C (addition of two hydrogen atoms): is the reverse process (reduction of the aldehyde back to the alcohol).

question: Some nitrogen-containing organic compounds behave as bases. Which of the following molecules is an example of such a compound?
explanation:
2-Aminoethanol (ethanolamine, HO-CH2-CH2-NH2) is a primary amine: the lone pair on nitrogen accepts H+, so it acts as a Brønsted base (its conjugate acid pKa is about 9.5).

Option A (isoprene): isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene), contains no nitrogen at all.

Option C (tetramethyl ammonium): the tetramethylammonium cation (CH3)4N+, has all four bonds on nitrogen occupied and no lone pair available to accept a proton; it is a permanently charged cation, not a base.

Option D (phenol): phenol, is a weak acid (pKa ~10): the aromatic ring stabilises the phenolate, so it donates rather than accepts H+.

question: All amino acids occurring in proteins contain at least four different chemical elements. Which of these elements DOES NOT occur in triacylglycerols?
explanation:
A triacylglycerol (TAG) is glycerol tri-esterified with three fatty acids; all three fatty acids and glycerol contain only C, H and O, and the ester linkages (–COO–) add no new elements. Amino acids universally contain N (in the α-amino group) in addition to C, H and O, and this nitrogen is precisely what TAGs lack.

Option A (hydrogen): are all present in the fatty acid chains, in the glycerol backbone and in the ester linkage carbonyls, respectively.

Option B (carbon): are all present in the fatty acid chains, in the glycerol backbone and in the ester linkage carbonyls, respectively.

Option C (oxygen): are all present in the fatty acid chains, in the glycerol backbone and in the ester linkage carbonyls, respectively.

question: Which of the following compounds present in the diet brings sulfur to the human body?
explanation:
Methionine is an essential amino acid whose side chain is −CH2-CH2-S-CH3, a thioether; together with cysteine it is the principal dietary source of sulfur for humans and is the sulfur donor of S-adenosylmethionine.

Option B (serine): serine, has a −CH2-OH side chain (hydroxyl, no S).

Option C (glycine): glycine, has only an H side chain (no S).

Option D (aspartic acid): aspartic acid, has a −CH2-COOH side chain (carboxylate, no S).

question: Purine nucleotides are broken down to uric acid in the human body. The accumulation of this acid in blood leads to a painful inflammation of joints called gout. Which of the following molecules breaks down to uric acid?
explanation:
Guanine is a purine (fused pyrimidine and imidazole rings); it is deaminated to xanthine and then oxidised by xanthine oxidase to uric acid, whose deposition in joints causes gout.

Option A (cytosine): are all pyrimidines (single six-membered rings): cytosine, uracil and thymine are degraded via ring reduction and cleavage to β-alanine (from cytosine/uracil) or β-aminoisobutyrate (from thymine), CO2 and NH3 — never to uric acid.

Option B (uracil): are all pyrimidines (single six-membered rings): cytosine, uracil and thymine are degraded via ring reduction and cleavage to β-alanine (from cytosine/uracil) or β-aminoisobutyrate (from thymine), CO2 and NH3 — never to uric acid.

Option D (thymine): are all pyrimidines (single six-membered rings): cytosine, uracil and thymine are degraded via ring reduction and cleavage to β-alanine (from cytosine/uracil) or β-aminoisobutyrate (from thymine), CO2 and NH3 — never to uric acid.

question: Which of the following molecules can be classified as a sulfur analog of an ether?
explanation:
An ether has the connectivity R–O–R'; replacing oxygen with sulfur gives R–S–R', a thioether (sulfide). Ethyl methyl sulfide (CH3-S-CH2-CH3) is exactly this structure.

Option A (methanesulfonic acid): methanesulfonic acid (CH3-SO3H), is a sulfonic acid, the sulfur analog of a carboxylic acid, not an ether.

Option C (diethyl disulfide): diethyl disulfide (CH3-CH2-S-S-CH2-CH3), has an S–S bond and is a disulfide, analogous to a peroxide, not an ether.

Option D (propanethiol): propanethiol (CH3-CH2-CH2-SH), has an –SH group and is a thiol, the sulfur analog of an alcohol, not of an ether.

question: Aromatic signaling molecules are synthesized from aromatic amino acids taken in the diet because the aromatic ring is rarely synthesized in the body. What is typical for aromatic compounds? They all

options.A: form cis / trans isomers labelled ortho-, meta- or para-
options.B: have only saturated bonds in the whole molecule
options.C: consist of at least one cyclohexane ring
options.D: contain a planar cycle with delocalized electrons

explanation:
By Hückel's rule, aromaticity requires a planar, cyclic, fully conjugated system with (4n+2) π electrons; the electrons are delocalised over the ring, which explains the exceptional thermodynamic stability, characteristic 1H-NMR ring current and electrophilic-substitution reactivity of aromatics.

Option A (form cis / trans isomers labelled...): confuses cis/trans (geometric) isomerism with positional isomerism: ortho, meta and para designate 1,2-, 1,3- and 1,4-disubstitution patterns on a benzene ring, not cis/trans.

Option B (have only saturated bonds in the...): aromatic rings contain formally alternating double bonds (delocalised π system), so they are not saturated.

Option C (consist of at least one cyclohexane...): a cyclohexane is fully saturated (no π system) and therefore not aromatic; the aromatic six-membered carbocycle is benzene.

question: Macromolecules containing monomers connected by O-glycosidic bonds include:
explanation:
Starch is a homopolysaccharide of α-D-glucose residues linked through α-1,4 (amylose) and α-1,6 (amylopectin) O-glycosidic bonds between the anomeric C1 of one glucose and a hydroxyl oxygen of the next.

Option B (albumin): albumin, is a protein whose monomers (amino acids) are joined by peptide (amide) bonds.

Option C (RNA): RNA, is a polynucleotide joined by 3'-5' phosphodiester bonds; although the ribose–base linkage within a nucleoside is an N-glycosidic bond, the inter-monomer bonds are phosphodiesters, not O-glycosidic.

Option D (galactose): galactose, is a monosaccharide and therefore not a macromolecule at all.

question: Choose the chemical reaction in which one of reactants is water:
explanation:
The conversion CH2=CH2 + H2O → CH3-CH2-OH is a hydration (Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond); water is one of the reactants.

Option A (hydrogenation of unsaturated fatty acids): hydrogenation, adds H2 (molecular hydrogen), not H2O.

Option B (neutralization of acetic acid): neutralisation of acetic acid (CH3-COOH + NaOH), produces water and sodium acetate; water is a product, not a reactant.

Option D (formation of an amide): amide formation (R-COOH + R'-NH2 → R-CO-NH-R' + H2O), is a condensation that also releases water rather than consuming it.

question: Which of the following substances contains more than one functional group?
explanation:
Glycerol (HO-CH2-CH(OH)-CH2-OH, propane-1,2,3-triol) carries three hydroxyl functional groups on its three-carbon backbone.

Option A (acetaldehyde): acetaldehyde (CH3-CHO), has a single aldehyde group.

Option C (dimethyl ether): dimethyl ether (CH3-O-CH3), contains a single ether linkage.

Option D (ethyl propyl amine): ethyl propyl amine (CH3-CH2-NH-CH2-CH2-CH3), contains a single secondary amine group.

question: What is the correct systematic name of HO-CH<sub>2</sub>-CH(NH<sub>2</sub>)-COOH?
explanation:
This structure is L-serine. IUPAC nomenclature makes the highest-priority group (–COOH) the principal characteristic group and numbers from the carbon closest to it: C1 = COOH, C2 = CH(NH2), C3 = CH2OH, giving 2-amino-3-hydroxypropanoic acid.

Option A (1-hydroxy-2-aminopropanoic acid): misnumbers by starting at the hydroxyl end (which would only be correct if OH were the principal group), and it also places OH at C1 rather than the terminal C3.

Option B (2-amino-3-carboxypropanol): wrongly demote the carboxylic acid to a substituent ("carboxypropanol" or "carboxylic… amine"), a violation of IUPAC priority rules where –COOH must be the parent function.

Option C (1-hydroxy-2-carboxylicpropane amine): wrongly demote the carboxylic acid to a substituent ("carboxypropanol" or "carboxylic… amine"), a violation of IUPAC priority rules where –COOH must be the parent function.

question: The most abundant ion of blood plasma is a sodium ion. For the full neutralization of its charge, it must form an ionic bond with
explanation:
Na+ carries a single positive charge, so complete charge neutralisation requires exactly one anion with a single negative charge: one Cl− gives the electrically neutral NaCl.

Option A (two chloride ions): (two Cl−) would produce a net −1 charge (NaCl2−, chemically implausible).

Option C (two potassium ions): is nonsensical because K+ is itself a cation and cannot neutralise another cation.

Option D (one calcium ion): one Ca2+, is again a cation and would double the positive charge instead of neutralising it.

question: The common form of iron in hemoglobin is the ferrous ion. Under some conditions it can be changed to the ferric ion by
explanation:
Ferrous ion is Fe2+ and ferric ion is Fe3+; converting Fe2+ to Fe3+ requires the loss of one electron, which is by definition oxidation. Physiologically this is what happens when haemoglobin becomes methaemoglobin, which cannot bind O2.

Option B (reduction): reduction, is the reverse process (gain of an electron) and would drive Fe3+ back to Fe2+.

Option C (dissociation): dissociation, refers to the breaking apart of an ionic compound in solvent, not to a change of oxidation state.

Option D (hydrogenation): hydrogenation, is addition of H2 to organic double bonds and does not apply to isolated metal ions.

question: What is the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> if for the neutralization of 10 ml of this acid 5 ml of 0.2 M NaOH is needed?
explanation:
H2SO4 is diprotic, so the stoichiometry is H2SO4 + 2 NaOH → Na2SO4 + 2 H2O. Moles of NaOH used = 5 mL × 0.2 mol/L = 1 mmol; the reaction consumes half as much acid, i.e., 0.5 mmol H2SO4 in 10 mL, giving 0.5 mmol / 10 mL = 0.05 mol/L.

Option B (0.1 M): (0.1 M) is what you get if you forget H2SO4 is diprotic and use 1:1 stoichiometry.

Option C (0.5 M): (0.5 M) confuses volume ratios or omits both stoichiometry and volume scaling.

Option D (0.01 M): (0.01 M) is off by a factor of five and does not correspond to any sensible calculation.

question: Ammonia, which is toxic to the human body, forms ammonium cation in aqueous solutions of body fluids. It follows from this that ammonia behaves like
explanation:
NH3 + H2O ⇌ NH4+ + OH−: ammonia's lone pair on N accepts a proton, which is the Brønsted definition of a base (Kb ≈ 1.8 × 10−5).

Option A (an acid): an acid, would donate H+ rather than accept it.

Option C (an oxidizing agent): an oxidising agent, would gain electrons; forming NH4+ from NH3 involves proton (not electron) transfer, so no oxidation state changes.

Option D (an amphoteric substance): amphoteric, describes species that act as both acid and base (e.g., water, HCO3−); in aqueous physiology NH3 behaves essentially as a base.

question: The pH of blood is 7.4, which corresponds to a concentration of 40 nmol/l hydroxonium ions. The concentration of hydroxonium ions expressed in mol/l is:
explanation:
The SI prefix nano- denotes 10−9, so 40 nmol/L = 40 × 10−9 mol/L = 4.0 × 10−8 mol/L, consistent with pH = −log[H+] ≈ 7.4.

Option A (40 × 10−15): (10−15) is the prefix femto-, six orders of magnitude too small.

Option B (40 × 10−12): (10−12) is pico-, three orders too small.

Option D (40 × 10−6): (10−6) is micro-, three orders too large.

question: How many times does the concentration of hydroxonium ions increase if pH decreases from 5 to pH = 3?
explanation:
Because pH is a logarithmic scale, [H+] = 10−pH and a decrease in pH of Δ(pH) corresponds to a 10Δ(pH)-fold increase in [H+]. Going from pH 5 to pH 3 gives Δ(pH) = 2, so [H+] increases 102 = 100-fold (10−5 → 10−3 mol/L).

Option A (2x): (2×) confuses the numeric pH difference with the concentration ratio.

Option B (10x): (10×) would correspond to only a one-unit pH change.

Option C (20x): (20×) has no physical or mathematical justification.

question: Synthetic reactions in our metabolism use free energy, often in the form of ATP. Therefore, from the point of thermodynamics they can be classified as
explanation:
Anabolic (synthetic) reactions have a positive Gibbs free-energy change (ΔG > 0) and are therefore endergonic; the cell drives them by coupling them to the hydrolysis of ATP (ΔG °′ ≈ −30.5 kJ/mol), which is strongly exergonic.

Option B (exergonic): exergonic (ΔG < 0), describes energy-releasing catabolic reactions such as ATP hydrolysis itself.

Option C (found at equilibrium): describes reactions at equilibrium (ΔG = 0), which by definition do no net work.

Option D (oxidative phosphorylations): confuses a specific catabolic ATP-generating process (oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria) with the anabolic reactions that consume ATP.

question: Gastric juice contains
explanation:
Gastric parietal cells secrete hydrochloric acid (HCl) via the apical H+/K+-ATPase (blocked by proton-pump inhibitors), bringing luminal pH to about 1.5–2.

Option A (chloric acid): chloric acid (HClO3, chlorine in oxidation state +5), is a strong oxidiser used in the laboratory and not present in the body.

Option C (hypochlorous acid): hypochlorous acid (HOCl), is generated in neutrophils by myeloperoxidase for microbicidal action, not by the stomach.

Option D (hyperchloric acid): "hyperchloric acid", is not a real chemical name.

question: For a detection of polysaccharides in histology microscopic slides the staining called PAS reaction is used. One of the chemicals used in this test is periodic acid whose formula is
explanation:
Periodic acid is HIO4 (iodine in oxidation state +7); in the PAS (Periodic Acid–Schiff) reaction it oxidatively cleaves vicinal diols of sugars to dialdehydes, which then react with Schiff's reagent to give a magenta stain.

Option B (HIO2): HIO2, is iodous acid (iodine +3).

Option C (HIO): HIO, is hypoiodous acid (iodine +1).

Option D (HI): HI, is hydroiodic acid, a simple hydrohalic acid (iodide, oxidation state −1), which is a reducing agent, not an oxidiser.

question: Body fluids differ in their pH values: pH of the cytosol is around 7, pH of blood is 7.4, pH of urine is around 5 and pH in the stomach is around 1.5. By comparing these values one can conclude that the lowest concentration of hydroxonium ions is found in the
explanation:
Because [H+] = 10−pH, a higher pH corresponds to a lower [H+]. Blood, with pH 7.4, has the highest pH among the listed fluids and therefore the lowest hydroxonium concentration (~40 × 10−9 mol/L).

Option A (stomach): (stomach, pH 1.5) has the highest [H+], not the lowest.

Option B (urine): (urine, pH 5) and.

Option C (cytosol): (cytosol, pH 7) both have higher [H+] than blood because their pH is lower.

question: The acidity of chemicals is described by the acidity constant, often expressed as pKa value. For example, the values of phosphoric acid are: pKa1 = 2.1, pKa2 = 7.2, pKa3 = 12.4. It follows from this that the phosphoric acid is

options.A: a strong acid that completely dissociates in water to phosphate and 3 H<sup>+</sup>
options.B: a very weak acid that is unable dissociate in water
options.C: an acid whose third dissociation step is the weakest
options.D: an acid whose third dissociation step is the strongest

explanation:
For any acid, a higher pKa means a weaker tendency to donate H+; pKa3 = 12.4 is much larger than pKa1 = 2.1 and pKa2 = 7.2, so the third dissociation step (HPO42− ⇌ PO43− + H+) is the weakest.

Option A (a strong acid that completely dissociates...): a strong acid would have pKa < 0; H3PO4 is only moderately strong in its first step and only partially dissociates in the second and third.

Option B (a very weak acid that is...): a pKa1 of 2.1 corresponds to a fairly acidic first dissociation.

Option D (an acid whose third dissociation step...): reverses the logic of pKa: the largest pKa denotes the weakest, not the strongest, step.

question: After mixing 600 ml of water with 200 ml of 8% glucose solution the concentration of glucose
explanation:
Mass of glucose in the starting solution = 200 mL × 0.08 = 16 g; total volume after mixing = 200 + 600 = 800 mL; the new mass/volume percentage = 16 g / 800 mL = 2 %. Going from 8 % to 2 % is a 4-fold decrease.

Option A (does not change): ignores dilution altogether.

Option C (increases 6x): confuses dilution with concentration and picks a factor that has no basis in the arithmetic.

Option D (decreases 8x): (8×) would only be correct if the final volume were 8 times the original, i.e., 1600 mL, which is not the case.

question: Calculate the molar concentration of sodium hydroxide solution whose mass / mass percentage concentration is 0.4 % and density = 1 g/ml. Molar mass of NaOH = 40 g/mol.
explanation:
At density 1 g/mL, 1 L of solution weighs 1000 g and contains 0.4 % × 1000 g = 4 g NaOH; moles = 4 g / 40 g·mol−1 = 0.1 mol per litre → 0.1 mol/L.

Option B (0.4 mol/l): (0.4 mol/L) mistakes the mass percent for a molarity and skips the molar-mass division.

Option C (0.01 mol/l): (0.01 mol/L) is off by a factor of ten (would require 0.4 g/L or 0.04 %).

Option D (1.6 mol/l): (1.6 mol/L) has no correct derivation and is likely from mistakenly multiplying rather than dividing by the molar mass.

question: In the lungs, this chemical reaction proceeds within red blood cells found in the blood:  HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>−</sup> + H<sup>+</sup> ⇌ H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> ⇌ H<sub>2</sub>O + CO<sub>2</sub>. If CO<sub>2</sub> is subsequently exhaled, the reaction will

options.C: convert more HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>−</sup> + H<sup>+</sup> to H<sub>2</sub>O + CO<sub>2</sub>
options.D: stop in the form of the molecule H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>

explanation:
By Le Chatelier's principle, removing a product (CO2 exhaled through the alveoli) shifts the equilibrium to the right, so more HCO3− + H+ is drawn into H2CO3 and dehydrated by carbonic anhydrase to H2O + CO2, replenishing the exhaled gas.

Option A (acidify the cell more): is the opposite of what happens: consumption of H+ makes the erythrocyte less, not more, acidic.

Option B (form more HCO3− + H+): describes a leftward shift and would occur if CO2 accumulated (as in the tissues), not if it were removed.

Option D (stop in the form of the...): is chemically wrong because H2CO3 is a highly unstable intermediate that carbonic anhydrase rapidly interconverts with CO2 + H2O; the reaction does not "stop" there.

question: The most abundant inorganic ions of human blood plasma are derived from sodium, chlorine, carbon, potassium, calcium, magnesium and phosphorus. These ions are
explanation:
Of the elements listed, Na, K, Ca, Mg are metals (they form the plasma cations Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) whereas Cl, C, P are nonmetals (occurring as the anions Cl−, HCO3−/CO32−, HPO42−/H2PO4−).

Option A (all metals): Cl, C and P are nonmetals.

Option B (all cations): Cl−, HCO3− and phosphate species are anions, not cations.

Option C (all nonmetals): is wrong for the mirror-image reason: Na, K, Ca and Mg are metals.
